user_input: What impact did the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) have on our unrecognized tax benefits as of December 31, 2025?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

Unrecognized Tax Benefits. As of December 31, 2025, we had net uncertain tax positions of $11.23 billion which was included in long-term income taxes on our consolidated balance sheets. These unrecognized tax benefits were predominantly accrued for uncertainties with our research tax credits and transfer pricing with our foreign subsidiaries, which include licensing of intellectual property, providing services and other transactions. The ultimate settlement of the liabilities will depend upon resolution of tax audits, litigation, or events that would otherwise change the assessment of such items.

See Note 14 — Income Taxes in the notes to the consolidated financial statements included in Part II, Item 8, "Financial Statements and Supplementary Data" of this Annual Report on Form 10-K for additional information regarding income tax contingencies.

Liquidity and Capital Resources

<2-hop>

On July 4, 2025, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (“OBBBA”) was enacted into law.  The OBBBA contains numerous business tax provisions, including business extenders made permanent such as restoration of 100% bonus depreciation, IRC Section 174 expensing for US-based research, and the EBITDA-based business interest expense limitation under IRC Section 163(j).  As a result of this legislation, Charter adjusted its current year tax deduction and remeasured its deferred taxes resulting in a current tax benefit offset by a deferred tax expense which resulted in a $547 million decrease to current income taxes payable and corresponding increase to deferred income tax liabilities.

Carryforwards
reference: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) enacted on July 4, 2025, had an impact on our unrecognized tax benefits as of December 31, 2025. The OBBBA resulted in adjustments to current year tax deductions and the remeasurement of deferred taxes, leading to a $547 million decrease in current income taxes payable and a corresponding increase in deferred income tax liabilities.

user_input: How might changes in laws regarding the use of cookies and similar technologies impact the operating income of an advertising business like Facebook's?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

The increase in North America operating income in 2025, compared to the prior year, is primarily due to increased unit sales and increased advertising sales, partially offset by increased fulfillment, technology and infrastructure, shipping, and other operating costs. Changes in foreign exchange rates negatively impacted operating income by $204 million in 2025.
The increase in International operating income in 2025, compared to the prior year, is primarily due to increased unit sales and increased advertising sales, partially offset by increased fulfillment and shipping costs. Changes in foreign exchange rates positively impacted operating income by $903 million in 2025.

<2-hop>

subject to interpretation, and may restrict our ability to offer certain products and services provided to all or certain cohorts of users in those states, adversely affecting our advertising business. In Europe, evolving interpretation of the ePrivacy Directive's requirements regarding the use of cookies and similar technologies may lead to regulators imposing specific measures in the future which could directly impact our use of such technologies. In addition, the ePrivacy Directive and national implementation laws impose additional limitations on the use of data across messaging products and include significant penalties for non-compliance. Changes to our products or business practices as a result of these or similar developments have adversely affected, and may in the future adversely affect, our advertising business. For example, in response to regulatory developments in Europe, we announced plans to change the legal basis for behavioral advertising on Facebook and Instagram in
reference: Changes in laws regarding the use of cookies and similar technologies, such as those evolving under the ePrivacy Directive in Europe, may restrict the ability to offer certain products and services. This could lead to adverse effects on the advertising business by imposing specific measures or additional limitations on data usage across messaging products. Such changes have adversely affected operating income in the past and may continue to do so due to potential penalties for non-compliance.

Coal
Section 4121 imposes a tax on the first sale of 
coal mined in the United States. The producer 
of the coal is liable for the tax. The producer is 
the person who has vested ownership of the 
coal under state law immediately after the coal 
is severed from the ground. Determine vested 
ownership without regard to any contractual ar-
rangement for the sale or other disposition of 
the coal or the payment of any royalties be-
tween the producer and third parties. A pro-
ducer includes any person who extracts coal 
from coal waste refuse piles (or from the silt 
waste product that results from the wet washing 
of coal).

The tax isn't imposed on coal extracted from 
a riverbed by dredging if it can be shown that 
the coal has been taxed previously.

Tax rates. The tax on underground-mined coal 
is the lower of:
reference: The tax liability for producers of sustainable aviation fuel credits involves compliance with IRS notices such as Notice 2023-6 and Notice 2024-6, which provide information related to the credit. On the other hand, the tax imposed on the first sale of coal in the United States is measured by Section 4121, where the producer, defined as the person who has vested ownership of the coal immediately after it is severed from the ground, is liable for this tax. The producer includes any person who extracts coal from coal waste refuse piles or from silt resulting from wet washing of coal.

user_input: WHY DID CHARTER RECEIVE A NOTICE FROM AN AND WHAT ARE THE CONSEQUENCES OF THIS NOTIFICATION?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

On August 4, 2025, Charter received a notice from A/N pursuant to the Existing Letter Agreement, whereby A/N notified Charter that A/N was suspending the standing share repurchase agreement between A/N and Charter (the “Suspension”). The Suspension took effect immediately after the first repurchase closing date under the Existing Letter Agreement to occur following the date of the notice.  In the notice, A/N informed Charter that it intends for the Suspension to continue through the consummation of the closing of the Cox Transactions or the termination thereof, but reserved the right to end such Suspension before or after such time.

<2-hop>

The general rules for determining U.S. source income 
that apply to most nonresident aliens are shown in Ta-
ble 2-1. The following discussions cover the general rules 
as well as the exceptions to these rules.
Tip: Not all items of U.S. source income are taxable. See 
chapter 3.

Interest Income
Generally, U.S. source interest income includes the follow-
ing items.

• Interest on bonds, notes, or other interest-bearing obli-
gations of U.S. residents or domestic corporations.

• Interest paid by a domestic or foreign partnership or 
foreign corporation engaged in a U.S. trade or busi-
ness at any time during the tax year.

• Original issue discount (OID).
• Interest from a state, the District of Columbia, or the 

U.S. Government.
The place or manner of payment is immaterial in deter-

mining the source of the income.
A substitute interest payment made to the transferor of
reference: CHARTER RECEIVED A NOTICE FROM A/N PURSUANT TO THE EXISTING LETTER AGREEMENT, WHEREBY A/N NOTIFIED CHARTER THAT A/N WAS SUSPENDING THE STANDING SHARE REPURCHASE AGREEMENT BETWEEN A/N AND CHARTER (THE “SUSPENSION”). THE SUSPENSION TOOK EFFECT IMMEDIATELY AFTER THE FIRST REPURCHASE CLOSING DATE UNDER THE EXISTING LETTER AGREEMENT TO OCCUR FOLLOWING THE DATE OF THE NOTICE. IN THE NOTICE, A/N INFORMED CHARTER THAT IT INTENDS FOR THE SUSPENSION TO CONTINUE THROUGH THE CONSUMMATION OF THE CLOSING OF THE COX TRANSACTIONS OR THE TERMINATION THEREOF, BUT RESERVED THE RIGHT TO END SUCH SUSPENSION BEFORE OR AFTER SUCH TIME. THIS NOTIFICATION DID NOT DIRECTLY RELATE TO U.S. SOURCE INCOME OR CERTIFICATES.

user_input: What are the specific conditions for a nonresident alien to qualify for tax treaty benefits, and how does this relate to the taxation of coal in the United States?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

Tax Treaty Benefits

Introduction
A nonresident alien (and certain resident aliens) from a 
country with which the United States has an income tax 
treaty may qualify for certain benefits. Most treaties re-
quire that the nonresident alien be a resident of the treaty 
country to qualify in the year the benefit is claimed. How-
ever, in the case of certain students, trainees, teachers, or 
researchers, some treaties only require the nonresident 
alien to be a resident of the treaty country immediately 
prior to coming to the United States.
Tax treaty tables. You can access the tax treaty tables 
by going to IRS.gov/TreatyTables. You can access the 
texts of recently signed U.S. income tax treaties, proto-
cols, and tax information exchange agreements (TIEAs) 
and the accompanying Treasury Department tax treaty 
technical explanations as they become publicly available, 
as well as the U.S. Model Income Tax Convention, at 
Home.Treasury.gov/Policy-Issues/Tax-Policy/International-
Tax.

<2-hop>

• It measures 5/16 of an inch or less in diame-
ter.

• It isn't suitable for use with a taxable bow.
Pay this tax with Form 720. No tax deposits 

are required.

Coal
Section 4121 imposes a tax on the first sale of 
coal mined in the United States. The producer 
of the coal is liable for the tax. The producer is 
the person who has vested ownership of the 
coal under state law immediately after the coal 
is severed from the ground. Determine vested 
ownership without regard to any contractual ar-
rangement for the sale or other disposition of 
the coal or the payment of any royalties be-
tween the producer and third parties. A pro-
ducer includes any person who extracts coal 
from coal waste refuse piles (or from the silt 
waste product that results from the wet washing 
of coal).

The tax isn't imposed on coal extracted from 
a riverbed by dredging if it can be shown that 
the coal has been taxed previously.

Tax rates. The tax on underground-mined coal 
is the lower of:
reference: A nonresident alien (and certain resident aliens) from a country with which the United States has an income tax treaty may qualify for certain benefits. Most treaties require that the nonresident alien be a resident of the treaty country to qualify in the year the benefit is claimed, but some treaties only require the nonresident alien to be a resident of the treaty country immediately prior to coming to the United States in the case of certain students, trainees, teachers, or researchers. Regarding coal taxation, Section 4121 imposes a tax on the first sale of coal mined in the United States, with the producer being liable for the tax and determined based on state law ownership immediately after the coal is severed from the ground.

user_input: What are the implications of USG's new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China on our AI-optimized infrastructure?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

At the foundation of our full-stack approach is our AI-optimized infrastructure — a key differentiator enabling us to power our own products, such as Search and YouTube, and support the services we provide to our Google Cloud customers. Our technical infrastructure allows us to use and offer our customers a range of AI accelerator options, including specialized Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and our own custom-built Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), such as Ironwood, our seventh-generation TPU. We are focused on driving efficiencies in our data centers, allowing us to leverage our technical infrastructure to deliver our products and services at an increasing scale while simultaneously enabling world-class research and model development.

<2-hop>

In October 2023, the USG announced new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China and Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5 (including but not limited to, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel) of our products exceeding certain performance thresholds, including, but not limited to, the A100, A800, H100, H800, L4, L40, L40S RTX 4090, GB200 NVL72, and B200. The licensing requirements also apply to the export of products exceeding certain performance thresholds to a party headquartered in, or with an ultimate parent headquartered in, Country Group D5, including China.
reference: The implications of USG's new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China on our AI-optimized infrastructure include potential restrictions on exporting products exceeding certain performance thresholds, such as A100, A800, H100, H800, L4, L40, L40S RTX 4090, GB200 NVL72, and B200. These restrictions may impact our ability to support the services we provide to Google Cloud customers in China, potentially affecting the scale at which we can deliver our products and services while also influencing world-class research and model development.

The increase in North America operating income in 2025, compared to the prior year, is primarily due to increased unit sales and increased advertising sales, partially offset by increased fulfillment, technology and infrastructure, shipping, and other operating costs. Changes in foreign exchange rates negatively impacted operating income by $204 million in 2025.
The increase in International operating income in 2025, compared to the prior year, is primarily due to increased unit sales and increased advertising sales, partially offset by increased fulfillment and shipping costs. Changes in foreign exchange rates positively impacted operating income by $903 million in 2025.

Tax Treaty Benefits

Introduction
A nonresident alien (and certain resident aliens) from a 
country with which the United States has an income tax 
treaty may qualify for certain benefits. Most treaties re-
quire that the nonresident alien be a resident of the treaty 
country to qualify in the year the benefit is claimed. How-
ever, in the case of certain students, trainees, teachers, or 
researchers, some treaties only require the nonresident 
alien to be a resident of the treaty country immediately 
prior to coming to the United States.

However, the direct impact of operating income due to unit sales and advertising on a nonresident alien's eligibility for tax treaty benefits is not explicitly stated in the provided context.

On July 4, 2025, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (“OBBBA”) was enacted into law.  The OBBBA contains numerous business tax provisions, including business extenders made permanent such as restoration of 100% bonus depreciation, IRC Section 174 expensing for US-based research, and the EBITDA-based business interest expense limitation under IRC Section 163(j).  As a result of this legislation, Charter adjusted its current year tax deduction and remeasured its deferred taxes resulting in a current tax benefit offset by a deferred tax expense which resulted in a $547 million decrease to current income taxes payable and corresponding increase to deferred income tax liabilities.

Carryforwards
reference: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) enacted on July 4, 2025, contains numerous business tax provisions but does not directly mention the impact on the Sustainable Aviation Fuel Credit. However, related information can be found at IRS.gov/irb/2023-02_IRB#NOT-2023-6 and other information related to the credit at IRS.gov/Credits-Deductions/Businesses/Sustainable-Aviation-Fuel-Credit for details on certificates and safe harbors. For updates, refer to Notice 2024-6 (Sustainable Aviation Fuel Credit, updated certificate and safe harbor) I.R.B. 2024-2, at IRS.gov/irb/2024-02_IRB#NOT-2024-6 and Notice 2024-37 (Sustainable Aviation Fuel credit, additional safe harbors), I.R.B. 2024-21, at IRS.gov/irb/2024-21_IRB#NOT-2024-37.

reference_contexts:

4.68		Form of 3.950% Senior Secured Notes due 2062 (incorporated by reference to Exhibit 4.5 to the Current Report on Form 8-K filed by Charter Communications, Inc. on October 12, 2021 (File No. 001-33664)).

In October 2023, the USG announced new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China and Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5 (including but not limited to, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel) of our products exceeding certain performance thresholds, including, but not limited to, the A100, A800, H100, H800, L4, L40, L40S RTX 4090, GB200 NVL72, and B200. The licensing requirements also apply to the export of products exceeding certain performance thresholds to a party headquartered in, or with an ultimate parent headquartered in, Country Group D5, including China.
reference: The new and updated licensing requirements announced by USG in October 2023 do not directly mention the Form of 3.950% Senior Secured Notes due 2062. However, these requirements apply to exports of products exceeding certain performance thresholds to specific countries under Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5 (including but not limited to Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel). The licensing requirements also apply to the export of products exceeding certain performance thresholds to a party headquartered in, or with an ultimate parent headquartered in, Country Group D5, including China. Since the Form of 3.950% Senior Secured Notes due 2062 is not specified as a product subject to these licensing requirements, it would be necessary to consult additional documentation for specific details on its export compliance.

In October 2023, the USG announced new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China and Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5 (including but not limited to, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel) of our products exceeding certain performance thresholds, including, but not limited to, the A100, A800, H100, H800, L4, L40, L40S RTX 4090, GB200 NVL72, and B200. reference: The new licensing requirements announced by the USG in October 2023 apply to exports to China, along with other countries in Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5. However, these requirements specifically relate to product performance thresholds rather than property use for business or rental income. For property used for business or rental income, the rules state that unless you owned and lived in a part of your property for at least 2 years during the 5-year period ending on the date of sale, an allocation of the gain on the sale is required between the residential and nonresidential portions using the same method of allocation used to determine depreciation adjustments. This information does not directly address the new export licensing requirements but provides guidance on property use for business or rental income.

In October 2023, the USG announced new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China and Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5 (including but not limited to, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel) of our products exceeding certain performance thresholds, including, but not limited to, the A100, A800, H100, H800, L4, L40, L40S RTX 4090, GB200 NVL72, and B200. subject to interpretation, and may restrict our ability to offer certain products and services provided to all or certain cohorts of users in those states, adversely affecting our advertising business. In Europe, evolving interpretation of the ePrivacy Directive's requirements regarding the use of cookies and similar technologies may lead to regulators imposing specific measures in the future which could directly impact our use of such technologies. In addition, the ePrivacy Directive and national implementation laws impose additional limitations on the use of data across messaging products and include significant penalties for non-compliance. Changes to our products or business practices as a result of these or similar developments have adversely affected, and may in the future adversely affect, our advertising business. For example, in response to regulatory developments in Europe, we announced plans to change the legal basis for behavioral advertising on Facebook and Instagram in
reference: The potential impacts of evolving interpretations of laws, such as those related to the ePrivacy Directive in Europe, can significantly affect the export of products under USG licensing requirements. For instance, changes in the interpretation of these laws may restrict the ability to offer certain products and services to specific cohorts of users, which could adversely impact our advertising business. Similarly, evolving interpretations of the ePrivacy Directive's requirements regarding the use of cookies and similar technologies might lead to regulators imposing specific measures that could directly impact our use of such technologies in Europe. These changes can impose additional limitations on data usage across messaging products and include significant penalties for non-compliance. As a result, we may need to adjust our business practices accordingly.

The general rules for determining U.S. source income 
that apply to most nonresident aliens are shown in Ta-
ble 2-1. The following discussions cover the general rules 
as well as the exceptions to these rules.
Tip: Not all items of U.S. source income are taxable. See 
chapter 3.

Interest Income
Generally, U.S. source interest income includes the follow-
ing items.

• Interest on bonds, notes, or other interest-bearing obli-
gations of U.S. residents or domestic corporations.

• Interest paid by a domestic or foreign partnership or 
foreign corporation engaged in a U.S. trade or busi-
ness at any time during the tax year.

• Original issue discount (OID).
• Interest from a state, the District of Columbia, or the 

U.S. Government.
The place or manner of payment is immaterial in deter-

mining the source of the income.
A substitute interest payment made to the transferor of

<2-hop>

uses.
• For use other than as a fuel in the propul-

sion engine of an aircraft.
• Exclusive use by a qualified blood collector 

organization.
• Exclusive use by a nonprofit educational 

organization.
• In an aircraft owned by an aircraft museum.
• In military aircraft.

See Sales by Registered Ultimate Vendors 
(Other Than State or Local Government Pur-

ultimate vendor claims.

Kerosene for use in aviation by a state or 
local government.  A state or local govern-
ment may generally not file an ultimate pur-
chaser claim for nontaxable use of kerosene 
used in aviation. Generally, only a registered 
credit card issuer or a registered ultimate ven-
dor may file a claim for kerosene sold for use in 
aviation to a state or local government for its ex-
clusive use (including essential government use 
by an Indian tribal government).
reference: The rules for determining U.S. source income related to kerosene vary based on its usage. For general U.S. source income, the place or manner of payment is immaterial in determining the source of the income. However, kerosene used in aviation by a state or local government does not qualify for a substitute interest payment claim and generally only a registered credit card issuer or a registered ultimate vendor may file a claim for kerosene sold for use in aviation to a state or local government for its exclusive use (including essential government use by an Indian tribal government).

user_input: What are the sources of U.S. source income for nonresident aliens, and how does this relate to managing credit risks in financial instruments?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

The general rules for determining U.S. source income 
that apply to most nonresident aliens are shown in Ta-
ble 2-1. The following discussions cover the general rules 
as well as the exceptions to these rules.
Tip: Not all items of U.S. source income are taxable. See 
chapter 3.

Interest Income
Generally, U.S. source interest income includes the follow-
ing items.

• Interest on bonds, notes, or other interest-bearing obli-
gations of U.S. residents or domestic corporations.

• Interest paid by a domestic or foreign partnership or 
foreign corporation engaged in a U.S. trade or busi-
ness at any time during the tax year.

• Original issue discount (OID).
• Interest from a state, the District of Columbia, or the 

U.S. Government.
The place or manner of payment is immaterial in deter-

mining the source of the income.
A substitute interest payment made to the transferor of

<2-hop>

Credit Risks
We are subject to concentration of credit risk primarily from cash equivalents, marketable debt securities, derivative financial instruments, including foreign exchange contracts, accounts receivable, and convertible notes. We manage the concentration of our credit risk exposure through timely assessment of our counterparty creditworthiness, credit limits, and use of collateral management. Foreign exchange contracts are transacted with various financial institutions with high credit standing. Accounts receivable are typically unsecured and are derived from revenues earned from customers located around the world. We manage the concentration of our credit risk exposure by performing ongoing evaluations to determine customer credit and we limit the amount of credit we extend. We generally do not require collateral from our customers.
Cash Equivalents
reference: The sources of U.S. source income for nonresident aliens include interest on bonds, notes, or other interest-bearing obligations of U.S. residents or domestic corporations; interest paid by a domestic or foreign partnership or foreign corporation engaged in a U.S. trade or business at any time during the tax year; original issue discount (OID); and interest from a state, the District of Columbia, or the U.S. Government. This relates to managing credit risks in financial instruments as it involves assessing the creditworthiness of counterparties, setting credit limits, and using collateral management for instruments such as accounts receivable and derivative financial instruments.

user_input: WHY DID CHARTER ADJUST ITS CURRENT YEAR TAX DEDUCTION AFTER THE ENACTMENT OF OBBBA?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

At the foundation of our full-stack approach is our AI-optimized infrastructure — a key differentiator enabling us to power our own products, such as Search and YouTube, and support the services we provide to our Google Cloud customers. Our technical infrastructure allows us to use and offer our customers a range of AI accelerator options, including specialized Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and our own custom-built Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), such as Ironwood, our seventh-generation TPU. We are focused on driving efficiencies in our data centers, allowing us to leverage our technical infrastructure to deliver our products and services at an increasing scale while simultaneously enabling world-class research and model development.

<2-hop>

On July 4, 2025, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (“OBBBA”) was enacted into law.  The OBBBA contains numerous business tax provisions, including business extenders made permanent such as restoration of 100% bonus depreciation, IRC Section 174 expensing for US-based research, and the EBITDA-based business interest expense limitation under IRC Section 163(j).  As a result of this legislation, Charter adjusted its current year tax deduction and remeasured its deferred taxes resulting in a current tax benefit offset by a deferred tax expense which resulted in a $547 million decrease to current income taxes payable and corresponding increase to deferred income tax liabilities.

Carryforwards
reference: CHARTER ADJUSTED ITS CURRENT YEAR TAX DEDUCTION AFTER THE ENACTMENT OF OBBBA BECAUSE THE LEGISLATION CONTAINED BUSINESS EXTENDERS LIKE RESTORATION OF 100% BONUS DEPRECIATION AND IRC SECTION 174 EXPENSING FOR US-BASED RESEARCH, WHICH LED TO A CURRENT TAX BENEFIT OFFSET BY A DEFERRED TAX EXPENSE RESULTING IN A $547 MILLION DECREASE TO CURRENT INCOME TAXES PAYABLE AND CORRESPONDING INCREASE TO DEFERRED INCOME TAX LIABILITIES.

user_input: Can kerosene used in aviation by a state or local government be claimed as an exempt use, and how does this relate to the emerging contaminants addressed in the review of photo-bioelectrochemical systems?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

uses.
• For use other than as a fuel in the propul-

sion engine of an aircraft.
• Exclusive use by a qualified blood collector 

organization.
• Exclusive use by a nonprofit educational 

organization.
• In an aircraft owned by an aircraft museum.
• In military aircraft.

See Sales by Registered Ultimate Vendors 
(Other Than State or Local Government Pur-
chases), later, for information about registered 
ultimate vendor claims.

Kerosene for use in aviation by a state or 
local government.  A state or local govern-
ment may generally not file an ultimate pur-
chaser claim for nontaxable use of kerosene 
used in aviation. Generally, only a registered 
credit card issuer or a registered ultimate ven-
dor may file a claim for kerosene sold for use in 
aviation to a state or local government for its ex-
clusive use (including essential government use 
by an Indian tribal government).

<2-hop>

Abstract
Emerging contaminants and their intermediate residue pose significant risks to human health and ecosystems due to their 
persistence in water and soil. The review explores the potential of photo-bioelectrochemical systems as a dual-function 
approach for the degradation of emerging contaminants while simultaneously generating bioenergy. Photo-bioelectrochemical 
systems integrate microbial metabolism with photocatalytic processes, utilizing solar energy to enhance electron transfer and 
promote the breakdown of toxic compounds. Recently, this technology has demonstrated improved removal rates of various 
emerging contaminants, including antibiotics and neonicotinoid pesticides, while producing valuable by-products such as 
hydrogen and methane through optimized operational conditions. Although studies have focused mainly on pharmaceuticals 
and dyes, applying these systems for pesticides remains underexplored. Algae-assisted photo-bioelectrochemical systems
reference: Kerosene used in aviation by a state or local government generally cannot be claimed as an exempt use for nontaxable purposes. Only registered credit card issuers or ultimate vendors may file claims for kerosene sold to such governments for exclusive use, including essential government use by an Indian tribal government. This relates to the emerging contaminants addressed in the review of photo-bioelectrochemical systems because although these systems are primarily focused on degrading various emerging contaminants like antibiotics and neonicotinoid pesticides, they could potentially be applied to address issues related to kerosene's environmental impact if such a need arises.

user_input: How did changes in foreign exchange rates affect operating income for North America and International regions, and what benefits might a nonresident alien from a treaty country qualify for regarding tax liabilities?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

The increase in North America operating income in 2025, compared to the prior year, is primarily due to increased unit sales and increased advertising sales, partially offset by increased fulfillment, technology and infrastructure, shipping, and other operating costs. Changes in foreign exchange rates negatively impacted operating income by $204 million in 2025.
The increase in International operating income in 2025, compared to the prior year, is primarily due to increased unit sales and increased advertising sales, partially offset by increased fulfillment and shipping costs. Changes in foreign exchange rates positively impacted operating income by $903 million in 2025.

<2-hop>

Tax Treaty Benefits

Introduction
A nonresident alien (and certain resident aliens) from a 
country with which the United States has an income tax 
treaty may qualify for certain benefits. Most treaties re-
quire that the nonresident alien be a resident of the treaty 
country to qualify in the year the benefit is claimed. How-
ever, in the case of certain students, trainees, teachers, or 
researchers, some treaties only require the nonresident 
alien to be a resident of the treaty country immediately 
prior to coming to the United States.
Tax treaty tables. You can access the tax treaty tables 
by going to IRS.gov/TreatyTables. You can access the 
texts of recently signed U.S. income tax treaties, proto-
cols, and tax information exchange agreements (TIEAs) 
and the accompanying Treasury Department tax treaty 
technical explanations as they become publicly available, 
as well as the U.S. Model Income Tax Convention, at 
Home.Treasury.gov/Policy-Issues/Tax-Policy/International-
Tax.
reference: Changes in foreign exchange rates negatively impacted the North America operating income by $204 million in 2025. For the International region, changes in foreign exchange rates positively impacted operating income by $903 million in 2025. A nonresident alien from a country with which the United States has an income tax treaty may qualify for certain benefits if they are a resident of that treaty country immediately prior to coming to the United States, as some treaties only require this residency status at the time of claiming the benefit.

user_input: How does the release of exosomes from small intestinal epithelial cells affect UCP1 expression and the aging process in adipocytes?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

ing the increased UCP1 and PGC-1α expression in young exosomes 
compared with control or old exosomes, normalized to GAPDH. E 
Images for β-galactosidase staining of 3 T3L1 cells. F Statistical 
results of β-galactosidase staining demonstrating the reduced relative 
numbers of β-galactosidase-positive cells in young exosomes com-
pared with control or old exosomes. G The levels of TNF-α and IL-6 
were detected by ELISA, showing the declined TNF-α and IL-6 lev-

sample was measured multiple times in technical duplicate and bio-

0.001

3 Biotech (2025) 15:163	 Page 7 of 10  163

<2-hop>

Discussion

Our study revealed for the first time that the aging of adi-
pocytes is closely associated with the release of exosomes. 
In this study, we found that the viability of 3 T3-L1 was 
increased and the degree of aging was inhibited when cells 
were exposed to exosomes of small intestinal epithelial cells. 
Changes in age-related features included SA-β gal activity, 
ROS activation, and SASP proinflammatory factors. The 
expression levels of miR-152-3p in the exosomes of small 

intestinal epithelial cells in young mice was distinguished 
from that in old mice, which affected the expression of 
PGC-1α and was related to the aging of adipocytes.
reference: The release of exosomes from small intestinal epithelial cells can influence UCP1 expression and the aging process in adipocytes. In our study, we observed increased viability and reduced degree of aging in 3T3-L1 cells when exposed to these exosomes. Additionally, young exosomes showed higher UCP1 and PGC-1α expression compared with control or old exosomes.

user_input: What are the key differences between Publication 519 (2025) and Publication 529, particularly in terms of their relevance to managing financial risks during an IRS audit as a Finance Manager?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

Married dual-status aliens can claim the credit only if 
they choose the nonresident spouse treated as a resident 
status, as discussed in chapter 1, or if they qualify as cer-
tain married individuals living apart (see Married Persons 
Not Filing Jointly in the Instructions for Form 8839).

Payments
You can report as payments against your U.S. income tax 
liability certain taxes you paid, are considered to have 
paid, or that were withheld from your income. These in-

reference: Publication 519 (2025) focuses on dual-status tax years for married individuals, providing guidance on claiming credits based on specific statuses or living arrangements. It discusses the treatment of taxes withheld from various sources of income, including wages earned in the United States and other items of income from U.S. sources. Publication 529, on the other hand, covers miscellaneous deductions that can be claimed by taxpayers, offering detailed information for various forms of income and expenses. Both publications are essential resources for managing financial risks and compliance issues related to intercompany transfer pricing during an IRS audit.

user_input: WHY DID MS. COLEMAN'S ROLE CHANGE FROM JANUARY 2021 TO MARCH 2025 AND WHAT WAS HER NEW POSITION?
reference_contexts:
<1-hop>

Liquidity
Our primary sources of liquidity include cash, cash equivalents, marketable securities, and cash generated by our operations. As of January 25, 2026, we had $62.6 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities. We believe that we have sufficient liquidity to meet our operating requirements for at least the next twelve months and for the foreseeable future, including our future obligations. We continuously evaluate our liquidity and capital resources, including our access to external capital, to ensure we can finance future capital requirements.

<2-hop>

Ms. Coleman was appointed Executive Vice President and Chief Human Resources Officer in March 2025. She previously served as Corporate Vice President, Human Resources and Corporation Functions since January 2021. Prior to that, Ms. Coleman served as Vice President Human Resources and Corporate Functions since September 2020. Since joining Microsoft in 2009, Ms. Coleman has held various positions of increasing authority.
Ms. Hogan was appointed Executive Vice President, Office of Strategy and Transformation in March 2025. She previously served as Executive Vice President and Chief Human Resources Officer since June 2023. Ms. Hogan had been Executive Vice President, Human Resources since November 2014. Prior to that, Ms. Hogan was Corporate Vice President of Microsoft Services. She also served as Corporate Vice President of Customer Service and Support. Ms. Hogan joined Microsoft in 2003. Ms. Hogan also serves on the Board of Directors of Alaska Air Group, Inc.
reference: Ms. Coleman's role changed from Corporate Vice President, Human Resources and Corporation Functions since January 2021 to Executive Vice President and Chief Human Resources Officer in March 2025.

user_input: What are the USG's new licensing requirements for exports of A100 products to specific countries and parties as of October 2023?
reference_contexts: In October 2023, the USG announced new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China and Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5 (including but not limited to, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel) of our products exceeding certain performance thresholds, including, but not limited to, the A100, A800, H100, H800, L4, L40, L40S RTX 4090, GB200 NVL72, and B200. The licensing requirements also apply to the export of products exceeding certain performance thresholds to a party headquartered in, or with an ultimate parent headquartered in, Country Group D5, including China.
reference: In October 2023, the USG announced new and updated licensing requirements for exports to China and Country Groups D:1, D:4, and D:5 (including but not limited to, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel) of products exceeding certain performance thresholds, including, but not limited to, the A100. The licensing requirements also apply to the export of products exceeding certain performance thresholds to a party headquartered in, or with an ultimate parent headquartered in, Country Group D5, including China.

user_input: What is the risk related to Amazon for the company?
reference_contexts:
•We are a “controlled company” under the corporate governance requirements of Nasdaq Global Select Market (“Nasdaq”), and intend to avail ourselves of certain reduced corporate governance requirements.
•The dual series structure of our common stock and the existing ownership of capital stock by our co-founders and their affiliates have the effect of concentrating voting control with our co-founders and their affiliates for the foreseeable future, which will limit your ability to influence corporate matters.
•Other factors discussed below. 
Risks Related to Our Business
Our revenue is dependent upon maintaining our relationship with Amazon and other marketplaces and our failure to do so, or any restrictions on our ability to offer brand partners’ products on Amazon and other marketplaces, could have an adverse impact on our business, financial condition and results of operations.
reference: Our revenue is dependent upon maintaining our relationship with Amazon and other marketplaces and our failure to do so, or any restrictions on our ability to offer brand partners’ products on Amazon and other marketplaces, could have an adverse impact on our business, financial condition and results of operations.

user_input: What changes did Instagram announce in response to European regulatory developments?
reference_contexts: subject to interpretation, and may restrict our ability to offer certain products and services provided to all or certain cohorts of users in those states, adversely affecting our advertising business. In Europe, evolving interpretation of the ePrivacy Directive's requirements regarding the use of cookies and similar technologies may lead to regulators imposing specific measures in the future which could directly impact our use of such technologies. In addition, the ePrivacy Directive and national implementation laws impose additional limitations on the use of data across messaging products and include significant penalties for non-compliance. Changes to our products or business practices as a result of these or similar developments have adversely affected, and may in the future adversely affect, our advertising business. For example, in response to regulatory developments in Europe, we announced plans to change the legal basis for behavioral advertising on Facebook and Instagram in
reference: In response to regulatory developments in Europe, Instagram announced plans to change the legal basis for behavioral advertising.
